entries.title: Monaco GP
entries.headline: Ferrari's big chance as FIA neuters Mercedes power advantage
entries.summary: Antonelli leads Russell by 43 points after four straight wins, but Monaco's tight corners and no Straight Mode zones could finally hand Ferrari their first victory of 2026.
entries.body:
Kimi Antonelli has won four races in a row now — including a dramatic Canadian GP where he and George Russell were scrapping hard for the lead before Russell's power unit gave up on Lap 30. That's extended Antonelli's championship lead to 43 points, and the kid's only 19. Russell bounced back in Canada by taking pole for both the Sprint and Grand Prix — beating Antonelli by exactly 0.068s each time — and won the Sprint, but the main race went south when the mechanical gremlins hit. McLaren meanwhile are looking to rebound after a costly strategic misstep in Canada that left them off the podium despite having the pace. Mercedes have won every Grand Prix this season but that streak's under serious threat here.

Ferrari's car has looked very strong in corners but lacking on straights, whereas Mercedes will potentially lose some of its power advantage in Monaco. Even better for Ferrari — the FIA has confirmed there will be no Straight Mode zones at Monaco this weekend, with both wings staying in high-downforce configuration throughout for safety reasons. Ferrari continued their strong start with Lewis Hamilton topping FP2 from Charles Leclerc, and qualifying on Saturday is absolutely everything here because overtaking is nearly impossible. Leclerc could well take his fourth pole in Monaco, while Hamilton will be buoyed by a best-ever finish in red, with second last time out in Canada. If either Ferrari nails it on Saturday, don't be surprised if Mercedes finally drop their first Grand Prix of 2026.

entries.title: Barcelona-Cataluyna GP
entries.headline: Barcelona’s Flashpoint: Antonelli’s Dominance, Ferrari’s Fightback, and Verstappen Under Pressure
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli arrives in Barcelona on a winning streak and leading the championship, with Mercedes setting the benchmark. Ferrari chase their first breakthrough win of the season, while Verstappen, McLaren, and the rest of the grid fight to stay in touch.
entries.body:
The story of the 2026 season so far has been the rise of Kimi Antonelli, and Barcelona is his next test as the benchmark driver in Formula 1. After winning again in Monaco amid chaos and restarts, he now leads the championship by a significant margin over Lewis Hamilton, who continues to be Ferrari’s closest challenger but still lacks a breakthrough win in red. George Russell sits third in the standings after a mixed run of results, but Mercedes arrive in Spain as the clear reference point for race pace and execution.

Ferrari’s weekend revolves around whether they can finally convert raw speed into a victory fight against Mercedes. Charles Leclerc, now firmly established as one of the season’s most consistent podium threats, has been outpaced in the standings but remains Ferrari’s sharpest weapon at a circuit that rewards precision and confidence in high-speed direction changes. Hamilton, meanwhile, is under growing pressure to turn repeated second-place finishes into something more decisive, especially as Ferrari’s development push intensifies.

Further back, Red Bull’s position is becoming uncomfortable. Max Verstappen has struggled to match the consistency of the front three and enters Barcelona needing both a clean weekend and a strong upgrade step to stay relevant in the title fight. McLaren sit in a similar zone of tension, with Oscar Piastri and Lando Norris trading results but still lacking the outright pace to consistently challenge Mercedes and Ferrari at the front.

Barcelona’s long, loaded corners will force everything to the surface — who has the best tyre management, who actually improved over the European upgrade cycle, and who is starting to fall behind. With the field converging and the championship already taking shape, this isn’t just another race weekend in Spain — it’s a clarity test for the entire grid.

entries.title: Austrian GP
entries.headline: Ferrari's Charge, Red Bull's Home Hopes, and Mercedes Reliability Fears
entries.summary: After Hamilton's Barcelona win, Ferrari rolls into Austria with a massive engine upgrade. Red Bull seeks a home breakthrough with a new package, while Mercedes faces reliability concerns.
entries.body:
Kimi Antonelli's championship lead took a hit after his DNF in Barcelona, but he's still sitting pretty with 156 points, though the gap to Lewis Hamilton is now just 41. Hamilton just snatched his first Ferrari win in Spain – a genuinely impressive drive that’s got the whole paddock talking about Ferrari’s recent upgrades and their ability to manage those hot conditions. Mercedes has to be a bit wary, though, with both Antonelli and George Russell retiring from strong positions in recent races due to reliability woes. McLaren has had their own share of mechanical issues but has shown flashes of pace that could see them fighting for strong points here. Meanwhile, Max Verstappen is still looking for his first win of the season, which is wild, but Red Bull is bringing a significant weight-reduction upgrade to their home race. 

The Red Bull Ring is a proper short, sharp circuit, demanding a lot from the cars with its big braking zones, elevation changes, and mix of high and low-speed corners – plus it’s known for being tricky on tyres. Ferrari's rocking up with a hefty power unit upgrade, rumoured to be a solid 8-15 horsepower gain, which should seriously help them on the straights and build on that Barcelona momentum, especially if the hot temperatures stick around. Mercedes, on the other hand, might find the heat and altitude a real challenge, given those recent reliability headaches and battery concerns. Red Bull, with their new weight-loss package designed to shed a significant 12kg penalty, will be gunning for a big result on their home turf, where they've historically performed well if they nail the setup. McLaren also has a good track record here, so keep an eye on Norris and Piastri if their car holds together. Off-track, there's buzz about Alonso and Sainz eyeing 2027 moves, and the FIA just confirmed some big changes to the 2027/2028 power unit regulations, shifting more towards the internal combustion engine to keep the racing tight.
